lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Today we're looking at 了 — le. It's an aspect particle, not a tense marker. English doesn't really have this, so don't translate it word for word.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Wait, it's not the past tense? I always thought 了 just means 'did something.'

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Close, but not quite. 了 marks a completed action or a change of state. It can appear in the present or future too, as long as something is finished by that point.
lines.ru_note: aspect particle — частица вида (показывает завершённость, а не время)

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 我吃了午饭。

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: See? 'I ate lunch.' The action of eating is completed. That's the first use — completed action.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: OK, I see. So what's the second use?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Change of state. It tells you something is different now compared to before.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 天气冷了。

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 'The weather has gotten cold.' It wasn't cold before, and now it is. That 了 at the end of the sentence signals the change.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: So there are two kinds of 了? One after the verb, one at the end of the sentence?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Exactly. Verb + 了 usually marks completion of that specific action. Sentence-final 了 marks a change of state or a new situation. Sometimes they overlap.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 我学了三年汉语了。
lines.pinyin: Wǒ xué le sān nián Hànyǔ le.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Here we have both. 'I have studied Chinese for three years.' The action started in the past and continues — that's why we use two 了 in this pattern.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: That's confusing. How do I know when NOT to use 了?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Good question. You don't use it for habitual or ongoing actions with no clear finish. 'I go to school every day' — no 了, because nothing is completed, it just repeats.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Right, so it's really about the result or the change, not just 'past.' I think I get it now.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Exactly. Think 'completed' or 'changed,' not 'past tense,' and you'll use it correctly most of the time.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Excuse me, teacher, can I ask about the homework?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Sure, go ahead.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: What exactly do we need to do?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Write ten sentences using 了, and read the dialogue on page twelve.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Got it. And when is it due?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: By Friday morning, before class starts.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Should I write it by hand or type it?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: By hand, please. It helps you remember the characters better.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: OK. And where do I hand it in?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Just leave it on my desk, or give it to the class monitor.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: One more thing — is it OK if I'm a bit late? I have another class right before this one.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: That's fine, just don't be later than ten minutes. Anything else?

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: No, that's all. Thank you!

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: No problem. See you Friday.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: Hi, welcome. Are you here to check in?

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Yes, I'm a new student. My name is Anna.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: Great, can I see your passport, please?

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Sure, here you go.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: Thanks. You're in Building 3, room 405. It's a double room.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: OK. Do I need to pay a deposit?

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: Yes, five hundred yuan. You'll get it back when you check out, as long as nothing is damaged.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Understood. Can I pay by card?

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: Card or phone payment, both work.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Perfect. What about the room key?

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: Here's your key card. It also opens the front door of the building.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Thanks. Are there any rules I should know about?

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: No smoking, no cooking in the room, and quiet hours are after eleven at night.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Got it. Thank you so much for your help.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: You're welcome. Welcome to the dorm!

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Let's practice measure words. In Chinese, you can't just put a number before a noun — you need a measure word in between.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: Like 'a piece of' in English?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Exactly that idea, but Chinese uses one for almost every noun. Let's start with 个 — ge. It's the general, all-purpose one.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 一个学生

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 'One student.' If you're not sure which measure word to use, 个 is usually a safe guess, though not always correct.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: What about books? I keep hearing 本.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Right, 本 — běn — is for bound things, like books or notebooks.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 两本书
lines.ru_note: 两 используется вместо 二 перед счётным словом

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: 'Two books.' Got it. And 张? I saw it on a sign.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 张 — zhāng — is for flat things: paper, tickets, tables, photos.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 三张纸

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: 'Three sheets of paper.' Makes sense, paper is flat.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Last one for today: 杯 — bēi — for cups of a drink.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: 一杯水

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: 'A cup of water.' So the measure word tells you the shape or type of the object?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Pretty much — shape, category, or how it's packaged. There's no shortcut, you just have to learn them with the noun.

lines.speaker: Student
lines.text: That's a lot to memorize, but the pattern helps.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: 你好！

lines.speaker: Server
lines.text: Hi there, what would you like?

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Um, what's this dish? It smells amazing.

lines.speaker: Server
lines.text: That's mapo tofu. It's tofu with minced meat in a spicy sauce.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Is it very spicy?

lines.speaker: Server
lines.text: Medium spicy. We can make it milder if you want.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: OK, I'll try it. And some rice, please.

lines.speaker: Server
lines.text: Sure. Anything to drink?

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Just water is fine, thanks.

lines.speaker: Server
lines.text: Got it. That'll be eighteen yuan.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: 多少钱？

lines.speaker: Server
lines.text: Eighteen yuan. You can scan the code here to pay.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Can I pay by phone?

lines.speaker: Server
lines.text: Yes, just scan this QR code with your payment app.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Done. Here's the confirmation.

lines.speaker: Server
lines.text: Perfect, thank you. Enjoy your meal!

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: 谢谢！

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Hi, I just got my student card. Do I need to activate it here for the library?

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: Yes, let me link it to our system. Can I see your card and your ID, please?

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Here you go.

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: Great, it's activated now. You can borrow up to five books at a time.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: How long can I keep them?

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: Thirty days. You can renew online twice if no one else has reserved the book.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: What happens if I return a book late?

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: There's a small fine, about half a yuan per day, per book.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: I see. And what are the opening hours?

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: We're open from eight in the morning to ten at night, every day except public holidays.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: That's convenient. Is there a quiet study area?

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: Yes, the third floor is silent study only. No phone calls, no talking.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Perfect, that's exactly what I need before exams.

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: Just remember to bring your card every time — no card, no entry to the study floors.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Understood. Thanks for explaining everything.

lines.speaker: Librarian
lines.text: Anytime. Enjoy the library.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Let's solve this quadratic equation together: x squared minus five x plus six equals zero.

lines.speaker: Li Wei
lines.text: Can we factor it, or do we need the formula?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Let's try factoring first. We need two numbers that multiply to six and add up to negative five.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Negative two and negative three?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Exactly. So we can rewrite it as x minus two, times x minus three, equals zero.

lines.speaker: Li Wei
lines.text: Sorry, what do you mean by 'move the term'? I heard that phrase in the textbook.
lines.ru_note: move the term — перенести член (уравнения) на другую сторону

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Good question. It means you take a part of the equation and put it on the other side of the equals sign, changing its sign. We'll need that for a harder example in a moment.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: So for this one, x equals two or x equals three?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Correct, because if either bracket equals zero, the whole equation equals zero.

lines.speaker: Li Wei
lines.text: What if it doesn't factor nicely? Like with weird numbers?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Then we use the quadratic formula. First we move the term so the equation equals zero, then divide both sides by the leading coefficient if it isn't one.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: And that part under the square root, what's it called again?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: That's the discriminant. It tells you how many real solutions the equation has.

lines.speaker: Li Wei
lines.text: If the discriminant is negative, there's no real solution, right?

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: Right, in that case the roots are complex. We'll cover that next week.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: OK, I think I understand the process now. Factor first, formula if it doesn't work.

lines.speaker: Teacher
lines.text: That's the right approach. Let's try one more example together.
